differences.title: 1. INITIAL LOAD
differences.leftDesc: Components render on the server, sending only HTML/text to the browser.
differences.rightDesc: Components send JS to the browser, then render, increasing load time.

differences.title: 2. INTERACTIVITY
differences.leftDesc: Can't use browser APIs directly. Ideal for static content or data fetching.
differences.rightDesc: Can use browser APIs and event listeners. Perfect for dynamic UIs.

differences.title: 3. DATA FETCHING
differences.leftDesc: Fetch data directly on the server, before sending to the client. No extra requests.
differences.rightDesc: Fetch data in the browser after initial load, requiring separate API calls.

differences.title: 4. BUNDLE SIZE
differences.leftDesc: Less JavaScript sent to the client, leading to faster parsing and execution.
differences.rightDesc: More JavaScript needed in the browser for hydration and interactivity.

differences.title: 5. SECURITY
differences.leftDesc: Sensitive logic and secrets remain on the server, reducing exposure.
differences.rightDesc: Logic runs in the user's browser, potentially exposing sensitive information.

differences.title: 6. REUSABILITY
differences.leftDesc: Components can be shared between server and edge environments for consistent rendering.
differences.rightDesc: Components are designed to run and interact within the browser environment.

differences.title: 7. STATE MANAGEMENT
differences.leftDesc: State is often managed server-side or passed down, reducing client complexity.
differences.rightDesc: Requires robust client-side state management for dynamic interactions.
